title: Module 2 Quiz
description: Test your understanding of identity, confidence, perfectionism, and self-talk.

blocks.content: Seven questions covering the core concepts from Module 2: The Stories You Tell Yourself. This is retrieval practice — one of the most effective ways to lock knowledge into long-term memory.

blocks.question: 1. Match each layer of the Identity Tree to its concept.
blocks.explanation: Correct. The tree builds from the ground up: roots (self-concept), trunk (self-worth), branches (self-esteem), leaves (self-efficacy). Each layer is more specific and more changeable than the one below it.

blocks.question: 2. Identity Foreclosure occurs when:
blocks.explanation: Correct. Brewer, Van Raalte & Linder (1993) showed that when athletic identity becomes the ONLY identity, vulnerability skyrockets. Injury or a bad season doesn't just hurt — it's existential.

options.text: An athlete develops deep expertise in only one sport discipline
An athlete's entire self-concept collapses into a single domain — being an athlete — with no other identity branches
An athlete retires from competition voluntarily
An athlete experiences normal pre-race anxiety

blocks.question: 3. Select ALL four sources of self-efficacy (Bandura).
blocks.explanation: Correct. Past performance, vicarious experience, verbal persuasion, and physiological/emotional state — ranked in that order of impact.

blocks.question: 4. The Teammate Test addresses which psychological trap?
blocks.explanation: Correct. The Teammate Test catches the double standard: brutal self-talk aimed inward that you'd never direct at someone you respect. Same honesty, more compassion, better results.

options.text: Maladaptive perfectionism — the double standard between how you judge yourself and how you'd judge a training partner

blocks.question: 5. Match each Performance Statement type to its example phrase.

pairs.right: "You've hurt worse than this and finished."

blocks.explanation: Correct. Technical = execution cue (body action). Pain Response = suffering counter-move (calm truth). Process = motivation for low days (reason to show up).

blocks.question: 6. A Schema Statement has two parts: '___' (strength) and '___' (aspiration).
blocks.explanation: Correct. The Athletic Schema Statement combines who you are (a demonstrable strength) with where you're going (a bold aspiration). Strength + aspiration. Read daily.

blocks.question: 7. An athlete who dismisses every compliment and fixates on the one thing that went wrong is exhibiting:
blocks.explanation: Correct. Dismissing compliments while fixating on flaws is the hallmark of perfectionistic concerns — Stoeber's toxic flavor. It's not high standards. It's a filter that rejects positive evidence and amplifies negative, leading to chronic dissatisfaction and burnout.

options.text: Healthy self-assessment and attention to detail
Maladaptive perfectionism — perfectionistic concerns that filter out positive evidence and amplify negative
